summary:
The weekly trends across these two distinct papers reveal a focus on **advancements in specialized AI/ML applications within the medical domain**, specifically targeting improved image manipulation and diagnostic explainability.

**Popular Directions:** Both papers emphasize **domain-specific refinement of advanced computational techniques**. The **OT-Bridge Editor** leverages a sophisticated mathematical framework (Entropic Optimal Transport) to tackle the challenging problem of *realistic, geographically controlled image synthesis* in medical imaging (CAG). This points to a trend of integrating complex optimization theory directly into generative models for high-fidelity medical tasks.

**Notable Advances & Shifts:**
The **OT-Bridge Editor** introduces a notable advance by framing localized image editing as a *constrained* transport problem, moving beyond basic diffusion models to achieve superior **precision and performance in feature synthesis** (stenosis).

Conversely, **Shapley Regression** focuses on **interpretable diagnostics**. Its advance lies in merging **game theory (Shapley values)** with regression to explicitly model **complex non-linear dependencies (co-occurrence)** among rare disease symptoms (APDS). This represents a significant shift toward developing highly **interpretable, robust statistical models** that can capture multi-way interactions better than standard linear predictors, while retaining desirable mathematical properties like convexity.

In summary, the week shows parallel work in **high-fidelity generative editing (via OT)** and **enhanced, interpretable predictive modeling (via game theory)**, both underpinned by tailoring sophisticated mathematical models to specialized medical challenges.

top_papers.title: Geometrically Constrained Stenosis Editing in Coronary Angiography via Entropic Optimal Transport
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces the **OT-Bridge Editor**, a novel method for synthesizing realistic coronary angiography (CAG) images with specific stenosis features. It reframes localized image editing as a **constrained entropic optimal transport (OT) problem**, using geometric information to precisely guide the generation process. This approach significantly improves downstream stenosis detection performance, achieving substantial relative gains on benchmark datasets compared to existing diffusion-based editing methods.
top_papers.figure_caption: Conceptual comparison between conditional diffusion and OT-Bridge Editor. OT-Bridge Editor fixes the start state S ∗ S^{\( \ast \)} (edited vessel-structure) and enforces hard, path-level guidance (GPG) within a Schrödinger-bridge formulation, producing a deterministic generation path toward the target.

top_papers.title: Shapley Regression for Rare Disease Diagnosis Support: a case study on APDS
top_papers.abstract: This paper introduces **Shapley Regression**, a novel game-theoretic model for rare disease diagnosis, specifically applied to APDS. It replaces the standard linear predictor with a $k$-additive cooperative game to explicitly model complex symptom co-occurrences, thereby improving upon traditional linear models. The method maintains the interpretability and convexity of logistic regression while demonstrating strong performance across biomedical datasets.
top_papers.figure_caption: Empirical Validation of Algorithmic Stability. We measure sensitivity (Euclidean shift ‖ 𝜽 ^ S − 𝜽 ^ S ′ ‖ 2 \|\( \hat \){\( \boldsymbol{\theta} \)}_{S}-\( \hat \){\( \boldsymbol{\theta} \)}_{S^{\( \prime \)}}\|_{2} after flipping a single label) across varying regularisation strengths C C . The dashed line represents the theoretical linear bound ( β ∝ C \( \beta \)\( \propto \) C ) derived in Regime 3. Crucially, the empirical points follow this linear trend in the high-regularisation regime (small C C ) but naturally deviate and saturate around C ≈ 1.5 C\( \approx \) 1.5 as the logistic gradients vanish. This confirms that the model adheres to the stability bound while being strictly more robust than predicted in low-regularisation settings.
